sentences.text: This was April 15 already, 1945.
We joined that group.
And as I've said, everything around us was burning.
And they were bombing all those areas around us.
Suddenly, we look around.
All the Germans, the guards, have disappeared.
Nobody with us anymore.

And the British prisoners of war laid out their shirts on the grass or on the ground, to tell the pilots, I guess, that-- some of them were pilots, I presume-- that those are prisoners of war in here.
And the planes kept getting lower and lower, just flying over our heads.
And I mean, the guys were screaming and yelling and waving.
And-- and I'd say about 10, 15 minutes later, the first American tank showed up.
And according to my research, it must have been Patton's army first.
And, of course, the guys picked that tank up, I mean, with their hands.
They surrounded that tank and-- the happiness.
Of course, we didn't speak English.
And they told us-- and it was interpreted to us-- that the American soldiers have just taken that particular village-- and I do not recall the name-- that we can go in there now.
It's all quiet in there.
So of course, we started going towards that village.
But on the road-- I've seen prisoners, I mean, die, and being killed, and being hanged.
But I've never seen, you know, soldiers in the fields with the trucks burning, and they're laying in and out of the trucks, and then-- and dead bodies all over.
And the stench was the only thing which was familiar, from the bodies.
We went to that village.
And of course, the Americans put us in a little home-- in a house.
And, of course, we already had food.
But while going there, I had a sack.
And I found some carrots on the road.
And I found some potatoes.
I didn't think-- I guess the mind wasn't working straight.
That is, couldn't be it.
And I put everything in my sack because I'm going to be hungry.
And I ate a carrot, a potato.
It didn't matter whether it was cooked or raw.
I mean, it was something to eat, a carrot.
And I kept on accumulating the things.
Of course, we came into a home, and we were-- we were-- we had some food immediately.
I remember one particular home.
That particular home, the basement had a lot of sausage in there.
And we were eating.
We were so starved that that night when we went to sleep, we have taken-- put a bench in front of our bed.
Put food out.
Every time we opened our eyes, or we just awaken, we would grab something and eat.
And not only this, but even if we had to go to the bathroom, we were eating and sitting [INAUDIBLE] at the same time.
I mean, this is how hungry we were.
Unfortunately, many, many, many of the prisoners, of the-- of the survivors have died after the war, by eating.
As I said, God still watched over me.
I-- I-- I-- I-- I-- must have chosen me to survive.
And I've done fine with it.
Even in one incident, I remember that there was a Jewish chaplain or someone, who found out that we were already liberated, found out we are former concentration camp inmates.
And he brought us a jar of peanut butter.
Now, we didn't know what peanut butter is.
And we designated my brother to be the cook for us.
We'd do everything else that has to be done.
But he's the cook to cook.
And he opened up the jar of peanut.
And he ate it.
And I presume it was good.
And he told us, this is not for you guys.
It's no good.
Don't you even touch it.
Don't even think of touching it.
And of course, a day later, it was empty.
And he had a good time just eating the peanut there.
And the guy brought us some canned-- canned, I think it was, gefilte fish.
And he took some pictures of us also at the time.
And suddenly, the-- the Germans decided to make a stand in there.
And there was fighting going on.
I thought it was going on for about two weeks.
But I could have been wrong.
But I thought there was-- we were right on the front lines in there.
And I believe this city was called Dessau.
It was someplace around Dessau where they've done this.
And this is this-- now after the liberation.
And we saw those truckloads of killed soldiers being brought back.
And they have also-- we were right with the kitchen.
You know, being hungry all your life-- for the-- for the last three, four years, that we were where the kitchen was.
Because the kitchen always had enough food to eat.
And this is the only thing which we cared about, is food.
So we were eating with the-- the guys on the front lines that they had all the other goodies out, cigarettes, as many as they wanted to.
Because those are the guys who went back to the front lines.
And I remember in one incident, a dessert.
And of course, we all had dessert, too, they gave us.
It was pineapple.
But I didn't know what pineapple was.
All I knew was that it was the greatest thing I've ever eaten in my life.
And when they get done with those big kettles, they going to dump the-- the juice.
I started screaming and yelling, are you crazy?
I mean, I've never had such nectar-- nectar, I mean, like this.
And we ran and we got a pail.
And they just put it into us in the pail.
Oh, boy, did we have a picnic with this.
I mean, how can you throw something like this out?
So we were looking at the soldiers and saying, what in the hell's going on?
The soldiers, I mean, eat, like, like, like, in the finest restaurants.
And then, as I said, the bombardments started again.
The Germans were resisting.
And I said, uh-uh.
I'm not staying here.
I'm going back.
In other words, they were shooting our way.
I was going back further.
I was going where the Americans were already liberated.
And I-- the only guy who was left in there was the guy from Belgium.
He said, I have all my confidence in Americans.
I'm not going with you.
I grabbed my sack of potatoes and carrots and whatever I had and I'm going.
As we-- as we got to the outskirts of town, I have never seen in my life so many tanks coming that way.
Tank after tank after tank.
And the American soldiers start throwing chocolate at us, cigarettes, and I said, what kind of an army is it?
I mean, they live on chocolate.
I mean, all we wanted, we ask for bread.
Brot, brot, bread.
Give us bread.
And they probably thought we said broad, give us a broad, you know.
But we wanted bread.
That's-- that's bread-- bread in-- in-- in-- in German.
And all they do is throw cigarettes at us, and chocolate.
And cookies.
Whatever it is in there.
Anyway, and they motion to us.
He says, go back.
Don't worry.
Go back.
Go back.
Some of them spoke a little German, too.
Go back.
We are here now.
You don't have to worry about it.
And we went back.
We went back to the same place where that-- David Stern from Belgium was.
He says, he says to us, I told you.
See?
Once the Americans are here, don't worry about it.
Anyway, unfortunately, this David took sick.
Very sick.
And of course, we figured also, I guess he had dysentery.
But he must have been one of the lucky ones.
The American army came and they took him to a hospital.
And we were not far from a city called-- or in a city called Kothen Anhalt.
I have a document from there someplace, which I cannot locate-- put my hands on it right now.
The document merely stated-- which regard that I needed something.
I wanted something to show that I'm a person, that I'm alive.
So we went to the magistrate-- magistrate in there.
And they gave us a little document which said that we were former prisoners in Auschwitz and we-- we were, without documentation, freed.
Bologna.
But anyway, this is what it said.
But I had something with an official stamp on it.
That city was a Red Cross city.
Almost every building was a Red Cross building.
And this was a hospital city.
So the following few days, we went from hospital to hospital, looking for David.
David was gone.
But while he was with us, and because we really-- he-- he-- he-- he says that we saved his life.
But that's besides the point.
So he's-- he always told us, if the war-- when the war will be over-- we know that it's going to be over-- that we should come to Belgium.
He said, don't go back to Poland.
Come to Belgium.
He says, I have a sister in Belgium.
She was hiding as a Gentile.
And I'm quite sure that she survived the war.
He says, I don't know it.
But I'm quite sure she has survived the war.
He says, because the Belgian people were wonderful people.
And we kind of listened to him.
So the first transport after the war that was going to Belgium, we had a little discussion, my brother and I and Joshua.
And we were-- decided we are going to sign up with the first transcript leading-- going towards Belgium.
And this was about May 31, May 30 when the transport left.
And again, it wasn't-- it was just boxcars again.
But they were open and clean.
And they stopped at every station.
We could get off, and-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We made that decision.
And we are in one of the cars, one of the boxcars.
And there's a gentleman, a civilian, from Belgium.
Says to us, you look like you're not from Belgium.
Couldn't speak French.
We couldn't speak Flemish.
So, he said, do you know anybody in Belgium?
Well, we do have a friend, but-- and I start telling him a story, my father was born there, but-- but-- oh-- I was born in Belgium.
Then my father had to leave and went to Poland looking for work.
He says, don't give me this cockeyed stories.
He says, I'm here to help you if I can.
Do you know anyone in Belgium?
So I told him about our friend, David.
We start talking about David.
He says, do you know anything about him?
I said, yes, we know everything about him.
He's been with us for a year or two, I said.
And, so he says to us, do you think-- do you know that he has a little limp?
I said, yes.
We know he has a little limp, which is really-- if you don't know it, you can't recognize it.
Because if they-- Germans would know that he has it, of course, he would have been in the gas chambers.
I said, but we knew that he had a little limp.
And he ask us which leg.
And I-- I don't remember now.
It's irrelevant.
He says, do you know how he got this?
And we said, yes, we know how he got it.
He says, how?
I said, he fought in the Spanish Revolution.
He said, my god, he said, you're talking about my comrade in arms.
You're talking about David.
I said, yes.
David Stern.
So he says, look, guys.
I will-- if he's alive-- and we told him the story, that he was freed.
We ran away together.
He was liberated.
He said, yeah, that sounds like him, he says.
He was a former partisan.
I said, yes, we know.
He says, if I can-- if he is alive, I'll find him for you.
So why don't you write a little note to him.
Well, we arrived in Brussels June 1, 1945.
And the reason also, it wasn't as much that we went to Belgium.
It's just that after living through the war, and I said to myself and my brother, too, that we lived only to take revenge and avenge.
Yet when the time came, we were pussycats.
We were just-- I-- I-- I witnessed where some Russian prisoners have just taken-- they said they knew the German.
And they have just laid him down, tied him up, and they just took a-- a regular saw and were just slicing them into pieces, working on him.
Well, I-- I couldn't do this.
And-- and-- and besides, all the German ground, to me, was like-- like-- like-- it was burning.
It was just, I couldn't be there anymore.
So, whether it was Belgium-- but since we knew that we might have someone in Belgium, we didn't want to stay in Germany anymore.
So we went to Belgium.
So we wrote a note in Yiddish, and gave it to him.
When we arrived in Belgium, they took us off-- the Jewish Joint Distribution Committee was waiting for refugees arriving.
And they took us immediately off there.
And we supposed to register, signing us up, and giving us shelter, home, this, that.
And suddenly, out of the clear blue sky, we see David.
Came to the train station.
And of course, we were so happy.
And-- and he told us the story that-- that he was coming to the station, to train station, every single day.
He says when he got better, an American air transport took him straight back to Belgium.
Because he was a former partisan, a former fighter.
He was known in Belgium.
And they took him straight back to Brussels, he says.
He says, he found his sister and he lives in a nice place.
And he-- and he says, the friend that you made on the train, he says, he saw me.
I was-- happened to be walking on the same sidewalk and he-- he recognized me.
He says, David, your friends are here.
He says, who?
He gave him the little note and he saw it was us, of course.
So he says, he tells us that I've been coming to this station, he says, every single day since my arrival.
Every time I knew that there is a train arriving, I came to see you.
So he went to the Jewish Joint Distribution Committee and he tells them, he says, from now on, they are my responsibility.
They're not going to go with you.
You don't have to worry about them.
I'll take care of them.
And that's exactly what he's done.
He had a place for us at his sister's home.
He wasn't even living there.
He had already his own place.
But it wasn't big enough.
So he took us there.
And there were three of us, my friend, and my brother, and I.
And we-- this is how we happened to have-- the reason why we happened to have arrived in Belgium and came to Belgium after the war.
Now, during that stay in Belgium, I'd say it was about-- we-- we did some work.
We-- we went to work for a certain-- it's-- it's like an American-- for the-- not occupational forces, because the Americans weren't occupying Belgium.
But they had-- they had-- they were making over some Eisenhower jackets, I guess, pulling them apart and doing something with it.
And we were working at that and earning already some money, too.
And then we were sent to the Ardennes mountains for rest.
So my friend Joshua and me, and I, we were there for a couple of days.
It was too quiet for us.
I mean, it was just mountains, a rest place.
We were already used to Brussels, I mean, a city, which was flourishing-- and streetcars, and cars, and theaters.
We-- we-- we decided to forego the vacation, and go back to Brussels.
My brother stood there all-- all the way through.
In about November of 1945, somebody came.
And you know, suddenly, you think of your families.
We were trying to find out-- we started writing home to Poland, to see if anybody else survived, if anybody came.
And we were corresponding-- the foreman in our brick factory was still there.
And the children, they send us back letters that nobody, so far, nobody came.
And then, I had a-- somebody came into a restaurant and told us that they think my sister happens to be in Frankfurt in one of those DP camps, Zalzheim.
They think it is my sister.
And the way they described it, it looked like my sister.
I immediately left for Germany.
As a matter of fact, I put on and American uniform, so it would be easier for me to get on a train and cross in there.
When I arrived in that particular camp, and from the second I arrived, I met that girl.
And unfortunately, I knew that girl.
She just lived 18 kilometers away from us, in another town.
And she was an-- a spitting image of my sister.
But it wasn't her.
So I immediately left back for Belgium.
We started looking, now, for relatives.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My brother remembered-- of course, we had an uncle in Costa Rica.
This I knew.
Because they were first cousins.
I mean, he was my uncle.
And all the kids left just before the war.
Because he usually went first there.
And then he send for his wife and children.
And they left, probably, 1938 or 1939, just before the wars.
They went to Costa Rica.
And we also knew that we had an uncle, my-- on my mother's side, who lived in Montreal, .
Canada.
We started writing there.
But we decided-- my uncle in Costa Rica decided to make out papers for us immediately.
And how did we get to Costa Rica?
We-- we-- we first written a letter from Belgium, I mean, when we finally were free people.
And Belgium treated us very kindly.
They were very nice to us.
Excuse me.
[DRINKS]
We started writing letters to all the relatives.
And we got answers.
We immediately started getting packages from-- from-- from Canada.
And from Costa Rica.
The letter was written to the American Joint Distribution Committee in the United States.
We wrote the letter to them, telling him that we have an uncle, Costa Rica.
His name is Jacob [?
Lewin-- ?] excuse me-- Jacob Kawer.
And he's there with his wife and family.
They-- and we wrote a letter to him.
They got it in New York.
Costa Rica is a small country, in a very small Jewish community.
And they send it to Costa Rica.
He immediately-- today, of course, you pick up a phone and call.
But at that time, in '45, you didn't do this.
He immediately got in touch with us.
And he immediately made out papers for us, for Costa Rica, before he even knew who survived.
He thought that my brother, and I, and my sister, the youngest one, would survive.
So made up papers in all three names, right away.
Unfortunately, my sister did not survive.
And my brother married a girl in Belgium, whose initials were also E.
Eva.
And my sister's name was Esther.
And now she's a Kawer.
So, decided, well, why change it?
E.
Kawer, and-- let's work it that way.
Anyway we went to the Costa Rican embassy.
And-- and-- and we talked.
And then somebody, for some reason or another, squealed to the embassy that she's not his sister.
She-- I mean she's not his sister, she's [?
Lazaro's ?] wife, my brother's wife.
So the counselor did not get excited.
He said, look, he says, it's much better for-- that way.
Costa Rica is a Catholic country.
You would never be able to have kids.
How can you have kids with your sister?
He says, it'll take a little bit longer now.
It'll take another month or so.
You'll get papers.
The visa has already been granted for one.
We'll just change it over to Eva Kawer, wife.
That's all.
And this is what happened.
So just before we about to leave Belgium, we discover we have relatives in the United States.
I did not know that we had relatives in the United States.
I would never knew the names.
I would never recognize anyone.
And-- although we had a relative on my mother's side in Brooklyn.
But I guess we never corresponded.
Or they've never written to each other.
So I never knew them.
Which, my mother had a brother in Brooklyn and one in [NON-ENGLISH], Montreal, and one in Uruguay.
Anyway, so when we left, we applied for a visitor's visa to see relatives in the United States.
Anyway, they gave us a visa here.
And we asked for a month, but they gave us two-- two weeks.
And the guy asked us, well, well, how much money do you have?
My brother says, $50.
He says, $50 for three people?
You expect to stay a month?
He says, we'll give you two weeks.
This was irrelevant.
We arrived here.
And those were my father's cousins, my father's first cousins.
There was a-- a-- a-- a-- one-- one brother and there were four sisters.
And one of the sisters lived in New York, so they took-- as a matter of fact, with the same name.
Same last name, Kawer.
She married a Kawer.
And anyway, so I-- I was left in New York for a little bit and then I came to Detroit.
I stayed in New York with-- with that particular cousin for about three weeks or so.
And then I came to the-- to Detroit.
And when I came to Detroit with the relatives, they were very, very kind.
They took me into their home just like their own children.
I mean, I was just an extra child in the house.
And I wasn't a child.
I was already a man, 21 years old.
But I missed so much in life, I was a child.
And they decided to try and keep me here.
They'll do everything possible so I can go to school, so I can go to college.
They said, we can't do nothing for your brother.
He is married, has a wife.
The papers are for Costa Rica.
So, anyway, they-- they have left for Costa Rica.
And I remained in here.
And I took a test.
I enrolled to-- with interpreters-- and I enrolled to college.
It was a private college called Great Lakes College.
And I was about the only foreigner in there.
And there were no Jewish kids in that school, except one Jewish professor.
And I had no choice but to learn English, fast.
I knew how to spell phenomenon before I knew how to spell love.
I mean, I-- I-- I just had to adapt myself very fast.
At this same time, I went to [INAUDIBLE] night school, just to catch a little bit of-- learn English street language.
I was getting all the highfalutin words in college.
And it just didn't work.
And I was also, at the same time, going to Wayne State speech correction clinic.
You know, tried to Americanize myself real fast.
It didn't help.
But anyway, I thought-- I thought I could lose my accent.
But it doesn't work that way.
Anyway, and I-- I went to school.
And I graduated with a bachelor's of art degree in business administration.
And then, for a while, while in school, of course, I worked for my relatives.
And I cost them a fortune.
I mean, they paid for all-- it's a private college.
But they all paid for it, which I am very, very grateful.
And I also, of course, started dating.
Oh, I started dating way before that.
But I-- on a blind date, I met my future, finally, one day.
I had a tough time convincing her to like me.
She didn't like me at the beginning.
But I guess I talked her into it.
I-- I guess I knew how to talk.
I used to call the house.
And she would tell her mother, tell him I don't like-- tell him I'm not home.
And she would say, in a loud voice, so I could hear, here is the telephone.
You tell him you're not home.
And of course, she had no choice but to talk to me.
My luck was that my mother-in-law, my future mother-in-law, which I didn't know at the time, she liked me.
I mean, she saw a Jewish guy, especially after talking, that I could read.
And I could daven.
This I said for his-- [LAUGHS] for him.
And so, anyway, we-- we dated for quite a while.
And we got engaged on January 18, 1952.
You-- you see, the-- the-- the-- the date 18 always was significant with me.
[INAUDIBLE].
So, we got engaged that time.
And we started planning a wedding.
And I had a problem with-- I wanted my brother to come to the wedding.
But if you recall at the beginning, what my brother's-- on his mind was always the-- the last words my father said is, keep Ben always with you.
And here, my brother's in Costa Rica.
And I'm in the United States.
So we started working on papers to get him into the United States.
And because we were working on the papers as a permanent-- as a permanent resident in the United States.
Because I was his brother.
And I wasn't an American citizen yet either.
But because of that, he couldn't come here.
Because they were working on his papers.
So he couldn't come as a visitor.
So I remember, at the time, we-- I wrote to Senator Moody as a-- as a-- he was a senator at the time, and telling him my story.
And this is the only living relative I have.
And I realized that the papers are about to come due for him to come as a permanent resident.
But my wedding is scheduled for the 26th of October, 1952.
Could he possibly make a-- an-- an exception, some sort of a dispensation, and have him come into the wedding.
And it did.
And he did.
And they allowed him to come into the United States.
And he came in.
He was the only really close person I had for the wedding.
And he was here for the wedding.
But-- and he went back, of course, there.
And-- but on his mind, still, was the last testament of my parent-- of my father.
He's got to be with Ben.
Ben is alone in the United States.
Well, he finally, the papers came through-- came through.
And he decided to arrive in the United States with his family.
My, of course, luckiest day was when I met my wife.
I think that was the greatest day of my life after the war.
I probably didn't know what living meant.
Because it wasn't just my wife.
It was the entire family.
They were a very, very fine family.
Also, the family that I lived with, they were just the greatest people on earth.
I mean, can you imagine when most of the-- the-- the refugees coming into this country had to go and work for a living immediately, I was about the only one which-- which was going to school.
I went to school for almost five years until I graduated.
First year, I mean, I had to get to know the language and everything else.
In-- as I said, during that time, I met my wife.
And again, this was my best day of my life, the best thing ever happened to me.
And my brother arrived from Costa Rica in 1953, to fulfill the promise made to-- that my father has said to him, always keep him with you, together.
So he came here.
Unfortunately, it just didn't work.
My brother got acclimatized to Costa Rica.
Work wasn't hard.
And if you wanted to work hard, he could have been a extremely wealthy guy.
When he came here, life is not so easy in the States.
He had to work hard.
He suddenly realized, hey, you have to work very, very hard to make a living in the United States.
Over there, he had maids in the house.
They even had a butler in the house.
And he was a plain, ordinary peddler.
Just peddling, from door to door, going and selling some goods from door to door.
And-- but he has made a beautiful living.
I-- I think when he came here, he already had about $20,000.
In 1953, $20,000 meant you were a rich man.
Anyway, I took him out one--
